images.title: Canada, carved by the Atlantic
images.caption: Canada Day
images.desc: On July 1, Canada celebrates its national day, marking the events of 1867, when the British North America Act (now called the Constitution Act, 1867) united three British colonies into one Dominion.

images.copyright: Giraffes at sunset in the Masai Mara National Reserve, Kenya (© danm/Getty Images)
images.title: Where shadows stretch tall
images.caption: Giraffes at sunset in the Masai Mara National Reserve, Kenya
images.desc: As the sun sinks low in the Masai Mara National Reserve, the silhouettes of Masai giraffes rise against a blazing sky and open plains. Another day comes to an end in the reserve, which stretches across about 580 square miles in southwestern Kenya, bordering Tanzania's Serengeti National Park. It is a vast expanse, one of the world's greatest wildlife arenas. Rolling grasslands, scattered acacia trees, and the life-giving Mara River set the stage for unforgettable encounters.

images.copyright: Bora Bora and its lagoon, South Pacific, French Polynesia (© Frederick Millett/Shutterstock)
images.title: Born on fire, held by water
images.caption: International Day of the Tropics
images.desc: Observed each June 29, the International Day of the Tropics spotlights a vast region stretching between the Tropics of Cancer and Capricorn, where warmth and rainfall shape daily life. This zone covers about 40% of Earth's surface area, yet supports around 80% of global biodiversity, making it central to planetary health and culture.

images.title: Looking sharp
images.caption: Saguaro cacti, Windgate Pass, McDowell Range, Arizona
images.desc: 'Stick around' takes on a new meaning in the Sonoran Desert, where the saguaro cactus has mastered survival. With trunks and arms raised in all directions, this giant has become the American Southwest's mascot.

images.copyright: Driftwood on Boneyard Beach, Hunting Island, South Carolina (© Frances/Adobe Stock)
images.title: The trees that lost ground
images.caption: Driftwood on Boneyard Beach, Hunting Island, South Carolina
images.desc: At Boneyard Beach within Hunting Island State Park, South Carolina, the first thing you notice is the trees. Then comes the question: why are bleached trunks and exposed roots scattered across the sand like a scene paused midway through collapse? Since the 1980s, Hunting Island has experienced major beach erosion, with some of the highest rates along the US East Coast—reaching up to 30 feet per year in certain areas.

images.title: Current affairs
images.caption: The River Thames, London, England
images.desc: What does a parliament, a clock tower, and a giant wheel have in common? They all stand beside the River Thames as if London had carefully staged its icons for dramatic effect.

images.copyright: Buildings on Grand-Place Square in Brussels, Belgium (© Vladislav Zolotov/Getty Images Plus)
images.title: Square up to history
images.caption: Buildings on Grand-Place Square in Brussels, Belgium
images.desc: Few squares in Belgium wear history as well as the Grand Place. The surrounding buildings, seen here, began as guild houses, each funded by a specific trade. Brewers, bakers, merchants, and craftsmen treated architecture as language, translating work, status, and rivalry into stone through reliefs, coats of arms, and generous gold leaf. Styles meet without caution. Gothic verticals rise beside Baroque curves, while the Town Hall, older than its neighbors and unapologetically asymmetrical, holds the composition together.

images.copyright: Butterfly pollinating on yellow flower (© lzh/Getty Images)
images.title: Pollen meets wings
images.caption: Pollinator Week
images.desc: Zoom in on the small transaction that keeps landscapes alive: a butterfly on a yellow flower, sipping nectar. While it feeds, pollen dusts its body and hitches a ride to the next bloom—one of the quiet ways flowering plants reproduce. Pollinators are not only bees. Butterflies, birds, bats, beetles, and many others help move pollen, supporting wild plants and many of the fruits and vegetables we eat.
